section.title: Two Kinds of Wisdom
section.sectionRationale: Having exposed the tongue, James asks the deeper question behind it: what kind of wisdom rules the heart? He sets two wisdoms side by side. One is earthly, unspiritual, even demonic, marked by bitter envy and selfish ambition and producing disorder. The other is the wisdom from above, pure and peaceable, bearing a harvest of righteousness. This is the answer to the plea for wisdom in chapter one and the root of the peace or strife that follows in chapter four.
section.thesis: There are two wisdoms with two sources and two harvests: a bitter, self-seeking wisdom from below that breeds disorder, and the pure, peace-loving wisdom from above that shows itself in gentle deeds and reaps the fruit of righteousness.
section.frameNote: Three movements: wisdom shown in humble deeds; the marks and source of false wisdom; and the character and harvest of the wisdom from above.

verses.text: Who is {{sophia|wise}} and understanding among you? Let him show it by his good conduct, by deeds done in the humility that comes from wisdom.
But if you harbor bitter jealousy and {{eritheia|selfish ambition}} in your hearts, do not boast in it or deny the truth.
Such wisdom does not come from above, but is earthly, unspiritual, demonic.
For where jealousy and selfish ambition exist, there will be disorder and every evil practice.
But the wisdom {{anothen|from above}} is first of all pure, then peace-loving, gentle, accommodating, full of mercy and good fruit, impartial, and sincere.
Peacemakers who sow in {{eirene|peace}} reap the fruit of righteousness.

sophia.gloss: Wisdom, the skill of living rightly before God, not mere knowledge or cleverness. James insists true wisdom is proven by conduct, by good deeds done in humility. The chapter distinguishes a false wisdom from below from the genuine wisdom that comes down from God.

eritheia.gloss: Selfish ambition, rivalry, the drive to promote oneself, possibly rooted in a word for a hireling working for pay. Paired with bitter envy, it is the engine of the false wisdom and the source of disorder and every evil practice. It is the opposite of the humility that marks true wisdom.

anothen.gloss: From above, from a higher place; the same word Jesus uses for being born again, or born from above. True wisdom is not generated by the human heart but descends from God, the Father of lights who gives it generously. Its heavenly origin explains its wholly different character.

eirene.gloss: Peace, wholeness, right relationship, corresponding to the Hebrew shalom. The wisdom from above is peace-loving, and peacemakers who sow in peace reap a harvest of righteousness. Peace is both a mark of true wisdom and the soil in which righteousness grows.

basic.context: James asks, who among you is really wise? Then he gives the test: not clever talk, but a good life and humble deeds. He warns that if your heart is full of bitter envy and selfish ambition, do not call that wisdom; it does not come from God at all but is earthly, unspiritual, even demonic, and it produces chaos and every kind of evil. By contrast, the wisdom that comes down from God is pure first, then peace-loving, gentle, willing to yield, merciful, fruitful, fair, and honest. People who make peace, and live at peace, harvest a crop of righteousness.
basic.hermeneutics: This is wisdom teaching that works by contrast, holding up two portraits so we can locate ourselves. The key is to see that James defines wisdom morally and relationally, by its fruit and its source, not intellectually. The list in verse 17 is not a checklist to admire but a description of a Spirit-shaped character, closely matching the fruit of the Spirit Paul describes. The passage also sets up chapter four, showing that the quarrels there spring from the wisdom from below.
basic.originalLanguages: The theme word is sophia, wisdom as the skill of godly living. The mark of false wisdom is eritheia, selfish ambition, paired with bitter envy. True wisdom is anothen, from above, the same word for being born from above, showing its heavenly source. And it is drenched in eirene, peace, the shalom in which the fruit of righteousness is sown and reaped.
basic.typology: The wisdom from above, pure and peace-loving, is embodied in Christ, the wisdom of God. The harvest of righteousness sown in peace anticipates the fruit of the Spirit and the peace Christ makes. The peacemakers James blesses reflect the Prince of Peace and echo Jesus' beatitude on the peacemakers, who are called children of God.
basic.christ: Christ is the wisdom of God in person, and the character of the wisdom from above, pure, peaceable, gentle, merciful, is a portrait of Him. He is the great Peacemaker who made peace by the blood of His cross, reconciling us to God. To have the wisdom from above is to be conformed to Christ, and to sow peace is to walk in His steps.
basic.god: God is the giver of true wisdom, which comes down from above from the Father of lights. His wisdom reflects His own character: pure, merciful, impartial, and peace-loving. He stands against the bitter, self-seeking wisdom of the world, and He blesses the peacemakers, who show themselves to be His children.
basic.crossReferences: The fruit of the Spirit in Galatians 5:22-23 closely matches verse 17. Jesus blesses the peacemakers as children of God, Matthew 5:9. Paul says Christ is the wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:24, 30. And Proverbs 3 describes the humble, peaceable path of wisdom.
basic.oneStory: From Proverbs to the cross, the Bible presents true wisdom as God's gift, culminating in Christ, the wisdom of God. James sets before the church two wisdoms and two harvests: the world's envious ambition that ruins community, and the heavenly wisdom that makes peace and grows righteousness. This is the wisdom promised to those who ask in chapter one, found fully in Christ, and it points to the peaceable kingdom where righteousness dwells.

context.literary: Verses 13 to 18 form the hinge of the letter's middle, flowing out of the section on the tongue and setting up the conflicts of chapter four. The structure is a clear contrast: a question and its answer in verse 13, the false wisdom in verses 14 to 16, and the true wisdom in verses 17 to 18, each defined by source, character, and fruit.
context.historical: James addresses communities troubled by rivalry, favoritism, and quarrels, as chapters two and four show. In that setting a wisdom that prized clever self-advancement would seem attractive, so James unmasks it as earthly and demonic, contrasting it with the humble, peaceable wisdom that heals a community.
context.canonical: The passage draws deeply on the biblical wisdom tradition, especially Proverbs, which links wisdom to humility, peace, and the fear of the Lord. Its portrait of heavenly wisdom aligns with the fruit of the Spirit and with Jesus' beatitudes, and it points to the New Testament identification of Christ Himself as the wisdom of God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To distinguish two rival wisdoms by their source, character, and fruit, to expose bitter envy and selfish ambition as a false, even demonic wisdom that breeds disorder, and to commend the pure, peace-loving wisdom from above that produces a harvest of righteousness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes the two wisdoms in order to prescribe a way of life: pursue and display the wisdom from above, and refuse the envy and ambition of the wisdom from below. The character list in verse 17 functions as both description of true wisdom and a call to embody it.

debates.question: Is the false wisdom merely worldly, or truly demonic?

views.label: A descending scale ending in the demonic
views.case: James calls it earthly, then unspiritual, then demonic, a downward progression. What looks like shrewd, self-serving wisdom is in fact aligned with the values of the fallen world, the unregenerate soul, and finally the demonic realm from which such envy and strife ultimately spring.

views.label: Strong language for its opposition to God
views.case: Others read the terms as forceful rhetoric marking how utterly this wisdom stands against God's, without necessarily implying direct demonic authorship in every case. Either way it is decisively not from above.

debates.bearing: Both agree this wisdom has nothing of God in it and leads to disorder and evil. James wants readers to stop dignifying envy and ambition with the name of wisdom.

debates.question: Why is the wisdom from above pure first, then peaceable?

views.label: Purity is the foundation; peace flows from it
views.case: James lists pure first deliberately: true wisdom is first holy and undefiled, and only then peace-loving. This guards against a false peace that compromises truth or tolerates sin; genuine peace grows from purity, not at its expense.

views.label: The order shows a whole, integrated character
views.case: Others take the sequence as descriptive of a rounded character in which purity and peaceableness belong together, with the following traits, gentle, merciful, impartial, sincere, filling out the portrait. The point is the wholeness of Christlike wisdom.

debates.bearing: Either way the priority of purity means peace is never bought by abandoning holiness; the wisdom from above is both pure and peaceable, in that order.

study.originalLanguages: The passage turns on sophia, wisdom as skilled, godly living, proven by conduct rather than claimed by words. The false wisdom is driven by eritheia, selfish ambition, joined to bitter envy, and James traces it down a scale from earthly to unspiritual to demonic. The true wisdom is anothen, from above, the very word for being born from above, marking its heavenly source in God. Its character unfolds in a chain of terms, pure, peaceable, gentle, accommodating, merciful, fruitful, impartial, sincere, and it flourishes in eirene, peace, the shalom in which peacemakers sow and reap the fruit of righteousness.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, the wisdom of God, whose character it describes, 1 Corinthians 1:24, 30.
Reflect the Prince of Peace and echo the beatitude on peacemakers, children of God, Matthew 5:9.
The fruit of the Spirit and the righteousness produced in those united to Christ, Philippians 1:11.

study.christ: Christ is the wisdom of God, and verse 17 reads like His portrait: pure, peace-loving, gentle, merciful, impartial, sincere. He is the great Peacemaker who made peace by the blood of the cross, reconciling sinners to God and to one another. To receive the wisdom from above is to be shaped into His likeness, and to sow peace and reap righteousness is to bear the fruit of union with Him. The wisdom the church lacks and asks for in chapter one is finally found in the crucified and risen Lord.
study.god: God is the source and giver of true wisdom, which descends from above from the Father of lights. That wisdom carries His own character, pure, merciful, impartial, and peace-loving, so that to grow in it is to grow like Him. He opposes the bitter, self-promoting wisdom of the world, which does not come from Him at all, and He blesses the peacemakers, owning them as His children. He grows a harvest of righteousness in those who walk in His peaceable wisdom.

crossReferences.note: The fruit of the Spirit, love, joy, peace, gentleness, closely matches the traits of heavenly wisdom.
Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called children of God, behind verse 18.
Christ is the wisdom of God, the ultimate source of the wisdom from above.
Wisdom's ways are pleasantness and peace, the wisdom tradition James stands in.
Filled with the fruit of righteousness that comes through Jesus Christ, matching the harvest of verse 18.

study.oneStory: The Bible presents true wisdom as God's own, given to His people and finally made flesh in Christ, the wisdom of God. James holds up two wisdoms and two harvests: the envious, ambitious wisdom of the fallen world that tears communities apart, and the pure, peaceable wisdom from above that heals them and grows righteousness. This is the wisdom promised to those who ask in chapter one and embodied in Jesus the Peacemaker, and it anticipates the new creation, the kingdom of peace where righteousness dwells.

quiz.q: How does James say true wisdom is proven?

options.text: By good conduct and deeds done in humility.
options.note: Verse 13: wisdom is shown in a humble, good life, not in mere claims.

options.text: By a life that visibly displays wisdom's character.
options.note: True to the verse, though less specific than humble deeds.

options.text: By clever speech and impressive knowledge.
options.note: James defines wisdom by conduct, not cleverness.

options.text: By boasting confidently about one's insight.
options.note: Boasting marks the false wisdom, not the true.

quiz.q: What marks the wisdom that does not come from above?

options.text: Bitter jealousy and selfish ambition, producing disorder and every evil practice.
options.note: Verses 14 to 16: envy and ambition are its signature and its fruit.

options.text: A wisdom that is earthly, unspiritual, and even demonic.
options.note: True to verse 15, naming its source rather than its symptoms.

options.text: Too much concern for humility and peace.
options.note: Those belong to the true wisdom, not the false.

options.text: A calm, orderly, and merciful spirit.
options.note: That describes the wisdom from above, its opposite.

quiz.q: Which list describes the wisdom from above in verse 17?

options.text: Pure, peace-loving, gentle, accommodating, merciful, impartial, and sincere.
options.note: Verse 17 gives exactly this Christlike character.

options.text: A character full of good fruit and free from favoritism.
options.note: True but partial; verse 17 lists more traits.

options.text: Shrewd, ambitious, competitive, and self-assured.
options.note: Those are marks of the wisdom from below.

options.text: Harsh, uncompromising, and quick to condemn.
options.note: The wisdom from above is gentle and merciful.

quiz.q: Why is the wisdom from above pure first, then peaceable?

options.text: Purity is the foundation, so genuine peace never comes at the cost of holiness.
options.note: The order guards against a false peace that tolerates sin.

options.text: Purity and peace belong together in a whole, Christlike character.
options.note: True to the integrated portrait, if less pointed about the order.

options.text: Peace matters more than purity, so we should compromise truth for calm.
options.note: The order teaches the reverse: purity is first.

options.text: Purity and peace have nothing to do with wisdom.
options.note: Both are central marks of the wisdom from above.

quiz.q: What do peacemakers reap, according to verse 18?

options.text: A harvest of righteousness, sown in peace.
options.note: Verse 18: peace is the soil in which righteousness grows.

options.text: The fruit of righteousness that comes from living at peace.
options.note: True to the verse, restating the same harvest.

options.text: Recognition and advancement for themselves.
options.note: That is the aim of selfish ambition, not the harvest of peace.

options.text: Nothing; making peace has no reward.
options.note: James promises a real harvest of righteousness.

questions: James says real wisdom shows up as humble good deeds, not clever words. Where in your life would others see wisdom by that measure?
Bitter envy and selfish ambition masquerade as wisdom but breed disorder. Where might that false wisdom be quietly at work in your heart or relationships?
The wisdom from above is pure first, then peaceable. Are you ever tempted to keep a false peace by compromising truth, or to defend truth without gentleness?
Which trait in verse 17, gentle, merciful, impartial, sincere, is God inviting you to grow in this season?
Peacemakers sow in peace and reap righteousness. Whom could you actively make peace with, and what would sowing peace look like there?

translationNotes.note: From above renders anothen, the same word used for being born from above; it stresses the heavenly origin of true wisdom over against the earthly, unspiritual, and demonic.
Accommodating renders a term meaning willing to yield or open to reason; the chain of traits reads as a portrait of Christlike, Spirit-shaped character.
A footnote gives the more literal rendering: the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace by those making peace, underscoring peace as both the manner and the soil of the harvest.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for James 3:13-18. No em or en dashes occur in this movement.
